Case study · Google · Nest Renew

Nest Renew: Emails

Helping users make a real dent in climate change, in the comfort of their homes.

Role
UX Designer (Contract)
Timeline
Jul 2021 – Jul 2022
Focus
Design system
Standard
WCAG & accessibility
Nest Renew turns a thermostat into a climate tool, quietly shifting energy use to cleaner hours and showing people the impact they're making, together. My work helped that story stay consistent from the wall, to the phone, to the inbox.
Collective impact · multi-surface
A note
This work is under NDA.
I can't show the inner workings of what I built. What's here is the public surface of the project ~ the problem, my role, and the system thinking behind it. I'm glad to go deeper in conversation.
01 — The idea

Climate action feels enormous and far away. Nest Renew makes it small, local, and yours.

Nest Renew is a service that enhances the Nest Thermostat, automatically leaning your home's energy use toward cleaner, lower-cost hours. For anyone who wants to make a difference but doesn't know where to start, it's a worthwhile, low-effort place to begin.

The challenge for me sat one layer out from the device: how do you keep that fluid, encouraging core experience intact as it travels into people's inboxes?

The question
How do you make addressing climate change easy and impactful — right from your home?
02 — My role

As the sole UX designer on the project, I built the design system, with accessibility designed in, not bolted on.

One designer, one system that everything downstream could lean on: a shared visual language, reusable patterns, and WCAG / a11y compliance treated as a requirement of the components themselves. The point wasn't a style guide, it was making the right, accessible choice the easy one for everyone who used it after me.

The system

A warm, optimistic visual language:
 green, hand-painted, and human, so clean energy felt approachable rather than preachy.

Color, painterly illustration, the Leaf motif and product photography, the kit every email was assembled from.
The building blocks

A library of modular content blocks, every message a person could receive, specced once and assembled like Lego.

Clean Energy Match, Carbon Avoided, the Leaf, Energy Shift, upsell, billing, monthly challenge, each a self-contained module with consistent structure, tone and accessible defaults, so any email could be composed from parts that already passed the bar.
Components

Down to a reusable icon set for the everyday actions that earn a Leaf.

One icon family, consistent weight and meaning, reused across emails and product, so a Leaf-earning action looked the same everywhere a person met it.
In practice

Then the system did its job ~ a fluid, accessible email experience across the whole lifecycle.

Waitlist to welcome, monthly impact, Leaf milestones, renewals, one coherent voice from the first email to the hundredth.
Milestones turn an invisible service into something you can feel proud of; a small, repeatable moment of progress that says your home did something good today.
Leaf milestone · 100 leaves
03 — Outputs

We launched the service and the system outlived my contract.

The team picked up the design system I developed and ran with it. Just as importantly, the accessibility practices I pushed for became part of how the Nest team works, my initiatives paved the way for a11y to be treated as a default, not an afterthought.

Service launched Design system adopted by the team A11y practices made default Visit Nest Renew ↗
04 — Reflection

Being the only designer on a project is its own kind of systems problem: you won't be in every room later, so the work has to carry your decisions for you. Building accessibility into the components, rather than asking people to remember it, was the highest-leverage thing I could do with one pair of hands.

A good system is generous. It makes the next person's accessible, on-brand choice the path of least resistance.
